justification: All pass criteria met: 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2, AC-3), already-shipped/delivered rejection (AC-4), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-5), plus authorization (AC-6), concurrency (AC-7, AC-8), audit (AC-9), and failure handling (AC-10). 5 low_confidence_areas and 4 escalations surface ambiguities including cancellation window, who can cancel, refund policy, and order lifecycle states. Confidence level of 42 is well below the 65 threshold. Edge_cases section is populated with 8 scenarios. Business rules are clearly marked as invariant vs. non-invariant, and assumptions are explicitly labeled as assumptions rather than presented as facts.

observations.text: Exceptional coverage: 10 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, edge cases, concurrency, authorization, audit, and failure modes
Confidence level of 42 accurately reflects the single-sentence spec — well below the 65 threshold
All three required ambiguities surfaced: cancellation window states, time restrictions (via escalation on order lifecycle), and who can cancel (sender vs recipient vs support)
Escalations provide concrete options with recommendations rather than just flagging problems
Business rules explicitly distinguish invariants from policy decisions, preventing invented rules from being treated as facts
Edge cases section is thorough with 8 scenarios including race conditions, refund service failures, and multi-parcel orders
BR-1 states 'Only the sender may cancel' as an invariant, but then correctly flags this as an assumption and escalates it — minor tension between the invariant label and the acknowledged uncertainty

BR-5 specifies a cancellation fee structure (full refund for Placed/Confirmed, partial for Assigned) that is somewhat invented, though it is marked as non-invariant and the refund policy is escalated as missing context
